perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall not and shall direct its Representatives not to, directly or indirectly, disclose, reveal, divulge, publish or otherwise make known any of the Evaluation Material to any Person, except as provided in Section 2(c) or Section 7 below. Except as otherwise provided herein, the Recipient shall treat the Evaluation Material as confidential at all times.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall endeavor to ensure that its Representatives do not, to a reasonable extent, directly or indirectly, disclose the Evaluation Material to any Person, except as provided in Section 2(c) or Section 7 below. The Recipient shall generally treat the Evaluation Material as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not' and 'shall direct' with 'shall endeavor to ensure' and 'to a reasonable extent' weakens the obligation, making it ambiguous. Replacing 'shall treat the Evaluation Material as confidential at all times' with 'shall generally treat the Evaluation Material as confidential' introduces further ambiguity, undermining the absolute confidentiality requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Law - Economic Espionage Act of 1996
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, by using 'endeavor to ensure' and 'to a reasonable extent', suggests a lower standard of care in protecting trade secrets than is typically required under the Economic Espionage Act. The law focuses on actively preventing the misappropriation of trade secrets, while the modification allows for potential leaks if 'reasonable efforts' are made, failing to meet the legal requirements for protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates strict confidentiality ('shall not disclose,' 'shall direct not to disclose,' 'shall treat as confidential at all times'). The changed text weakens this obligation by using terms like 'endeavor to ensure,' 'to a reasonable extent,' and 'generally treat as confidential,' which introduces ambiguity and lowers the standard of care. The Law Explanation asserts that this weakening fails to meet the legal requirements for protecting trade secrets, as it allows for potential leaks if only 'reasonable efforts' are made. The Economic Espionage Act of 1996, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, focuses on actively preventing misappropriation, a standard undermined by the modified contract's ambiguous language.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall, and shall direct its Representatives to, use the Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating, negotiating or consummating the Proposed Transaction in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall, and shall encourage its Representatives to, use the Evaluation Material primarily for the purpose of evaluating, negotiating or consummating the Proposed Transaction, in a manner generally consistent with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall direct' with 'shall encourage' weakens the directive. 'Solely' is replaced with 'primarily,' and 'in accordance with' is replaced with 'in a manner generally consistent with,' introducing ambiguity about the permitted use of the Evaluation Material and allowing for potential deviations from the agreement's terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section relates to agreements that must be in writing and signed. By introducing the ambiguity of 'primarily' and 'generally consistent with,' the altered text could allow for interpretations that undermine the original intent of restricting usage solely for the proposed transaction and strictly adhering to the agreement's terms, potentially rendering the agreement less enforceable under New York law if disputes arise regarding usage outside the explicitly stated purpose.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall direct' with 'shall encourage,' 'solely' with 'primarily,' and 'in accordance with' with 'in a manner generally consistent with.' This weakening undermines the original intent of strict adherence and sole usage, potentially making the agreement less enforceable under New York law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, NYS General Obligations Law § 5-701, emphasizes the necessity of written agreements, especially concerning contractual obligations. The altered text introduces ambiguity ('primarily,' 'generally consistent'), which could lead to disputes about the contract's enforceability if interpretations diverge from the original intent of strictly limiting use to the proposed transaction, therefore creating a contradiction because ambiguity undermines the clarity required for enforceable written agreements.

perturbation.original_text: As promptly as practicable following the written request of the Disclosing Party (but in any event within seven (7) calendar days), the Recipient shall, and shall direct its Representatives to, destroy all Evaluation Material in tangible form (whether in written form, electronically stored or otherwise) furnished to Recipient and in Recipient’s possession or in the possession of any of its Representatives, and neither the Recipient nor any of its Representatives shall retain any copies thereof, except to the extent required to comply with applicable Law or bona fide internal record retention policies or procedures for legal, compliance or regulatory purposes
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall, within a reasonable timeframe following a written request from the Disclosing Party, make a good faith effort to destroy Evaluation Material in tangible form. The Recipient and its Representatives may retain copies as deemed necessary for internal purposes.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the strict timeline ('as promptly as practicable' and 'within seven calendar days') with 'reasonable timeframe', which is subjective. The mandatory 'shall destroy' becomes 'make a good faith effort to destroy', weakening the obligation. Removing the specific exceptions and replacing them with the blanket 'as deemed necessary for internal purposes' allows for broad retention, contradicting the original intent of limited retention.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act addresses the destruction or alteration of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy. The original clause ensures specific destruction of evaluation material, but the modified clause provides leeway. While SOX primarily deals with the destruction of documents to impede federal investigations, broad interpretation of the clause 'may retain copies as deemed necessary for internal purposes' could lead to situations where important documents are retained, making it difficult to determine what constitutes normal 'internal purposes' versus obstruction of potential investigations relating to the disclosed information. Though not directly related, SOX and regulations under it require specific controls in public firms for document retention.

Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy Whoever knowingly alters, destroys, mutilates, conceals, covers up, falsifies, or makes a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matt
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the obligation to destroy evaluation material, potentially conflicting with laws against obstructing justice, such as 18 USC 1519. The original text mandates destruction within a specific timeframe, while the changed text only requires a 'good faith effort' within a 'reasonable timeframe', which is ambiguous. The altered text allows retention 'as deemed necessary for internal purposes,' potentially conflicting with the intent to prevent obstruction of investigations by retaining documents that should have been destroyed. 18 USC 1519 prohibits knowingly altering or destroying records with the intent to impede an investigation; the modified clause's ambiguity could be exploited to retain documents relevant to potential investigations, making it difficult to determine whether the retention is for legitimate internal purposes or to obstruct justice.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the Disclosing Party and the Recipient hereby (i) agrees that any lawsuit, proceeding or action with respect to this Agreement may be brought only in the courts of the State of New York sitting in the Borough of Manhattan of the City of New York or of the United States of America for the Southern District of New York, (ii) accepts for itself and in respect of its property, generally and unconditionally, the exclusive jurisdiction of such courts, (iii) irrevocably waives any objection, including, without limitation, any objection to the laying of venue or based on the grounds of forum non conveniens , which it may now or hereafter have to the bringing of any lawsuit, proceeding or action in those jurisdictions, and (iv) irrevocably consents to the service of process of any of the courts referred to above in any lawsuit, proceeding or action by the mailing of copies of the process to the parties hereto as provided in clause (a) above.
perturbation.changed_text: The Disclosing Party and the Recipient agree that lawsuits related to this Agreement should ideally be brought in New York courts. They generally accept the jurisdiction of these courts and intend to waive objections to venue to the extent permissible. They consent to service of process by mail, where practical.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces definitive language with suggestions: 'agrees that... may be brought only' becomes 'should ideally be brought'; 'accepts... unconditionally' becomes 'generally accept'; 'irrevocably waives' becomes 'intend to waive... to the extent permissible'; and 'irrevocably consents' becomes 'consent... where practical'. These changes introduce significant ambiguity, allowing parties to potentially challenge jurisdiction and service of process, undermining the original intent of a clear forum selection clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute pertains to the change of venue. While parties can agree to a forum selection clause, the watered-down version makes it more open to challenge. The original clause intended to make the venue specific and indisputable but changing the language opens the door for one of the parties to request that a case be transferred to another venue, potentially resulting in increased litigation costs and complexity of the matter. Therefore, the modified text makes it easier to use Section 1404 to move venue despite the initial forum selection clause.

Change of venue (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in t
perturbation.justification: The original text contains definitive agreement to venue, whereas the modified text introduces ambiguity, allowing parties to potentially challenge jurisdiction. The original text says lawsuits 'may be brought only' in NY courts, the parties 'unconditionally' accept the jurisdiction of those courts, and they 'irrevocably' waive objections. The modified text waters this down to lawsuits 'should ideally be brought' in NY, the parties 'generally accept' jurisdiction, and 'intend to waive...to the extent permissible'. The Law Explanation notes that this change makes it easier to use Section 1404 (Change of Venue) to move the venue, despite the initial forum selection clause, increasing litigation costs. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 28 USC 1404, states that district courts may transfer civil actions to other districts for the convenience of parties and witnesses and in the interest of justice. The changed text undermines the original intent of a clear forum selection clause, and creates Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.
